title: The First Quarrel
content:
I.
ï¿½Wait a little,ï¿½ you say, ï¿½you are sure it ï¿½ll all come right,ï¿½
But the boy was born iï¿½ trouble, anï¿½ looks so wan anï¿½ so white:
Wait! anï¿½ once I haï¿½ waitedï¿½I hadnï¿½t to wait for long.
Now I wait, wait, wait for Harry.ï¿½No, no, you are doing me wrong!
Harry and I were married: the boy can hold up his head,
The boy was born in wedlock, but after my man was dead;
I haï¿½ workï¿½d for him fifteen years, anï¿½ I work anï¿½ I wait to the end.
I am all alone in the world, anï¿½ you are my only friend.

II.
Doctor, if you can wait, Iï¿½ll tell you the tale oï¿½ my life.
When Harry anï¿½ I were children, he callï¿½d me his own little wife;
I was happy when I was with him, anï¿½ sorry when he was away.
Anï¿½ when we playï¿½d together, I loved him better than play;
He workt me the daisy chainï¿½he made me the cowslip ball,
He fought the boys that were rude, anï¿½ I loved him better than all.
Passionate girl thoï¿½ I was, anï¿½ often at home in disgrace,
I never could quarrel with Harryï¿½I had but to look in his face.

III.
There was a farmer in Dorset of Harryï¿½s kin, that had need
Of a good stout lad at his farm; he sent, anï¿½ the father agreed;
So Harry was bound to the Dorsetshire farm for years anï¿½ for years;
I walked with him down to the quay, poor lad, anï¿½ we parted in tears.
The boat was beginning to move, we heard them a-ringing the bell,
Iï¿½ll never love any but you, God bless you, my own little Nell.ï¿½

IV.
I was a child, anï¿½ he was a child, anï¿½ he came to harm;
There was a girl, a hussy, that workt with him up at the farm,
One had deceived her anï¿½ left her alone with her sin anï¿½ her shame,
And so she was wicked with Harry; the girl was the most to blame.

V.
And years went over till I that was little had grown so tall,
The men would say of the maids, ï¿½Our Nellyï¿½s the flower of ï¿½em all.ï¿½
I didnï¿½t take heed oï¿½ them, but I taught myself all I could
To make a good wife for Harry, when Harry came home for good.

VI.
Often I seemï¿½d unhappy, and often as happy too,
For I heard it abroad in the fields ï¿½Iï¿½ll never love any but you;ï¿½
ï¿½Iï¿½ll never love any but youï¿½ the morning song of the lark,
ï¿½Iï¿½11 never love any but youï¿½ the nightin galeï¿½s hymn in the dark.

VII.
And Harry came home at last, but he lookï¿½d at me sidelong and shy,
Vext me a bit, till he told me that so many years had gone by,
I had grown so handsome and tallï¿½that I might haï¿½ forgot him somehowï¿½
For he thoughtï¿½there were other ladsï¿½he was fearï¿½d to look at me now.

VIII.
Hard was the frost in the field, we were married oï¿½ Christmas day,
Married among the red berries, anï¿½ all as merry as Mayï¿½
Those were the pleasant times, my house anï¿½ my man were my pride,
We seemï¿½d like ships iï¿½ the Channel a-sailing with wind anï¿½ tide.

IX.
But work was scant in the Isle, thoï¿½ he tried the villages round,
So Harry went over the Solent to see if work could be found;
Anï¿½ he wrote ï¿½I haï¿½ six weeksï¿½ work, little wife, so far as I know;
Iï¿½ll come for an hour to-morrow, anï¿½ kiss you before I go.ï¿½

X.
So I set to righting the house, for wasnï¿½t he coming that day?
Anï¿½ I hit on an old deal-box that was pasted in a corner away,
It was full of old odds anï¿½ ends, anï¿½ a letter along wiï¿½ the rest,
I had better haï¿½ put my naked hand in a hornetsï¿½ nest.

XI.
ï¿½Sweetheartï¿½ï¿½this was the letterï¿½this was the letter I readï¿½
ï¿½You promised to find me work near you, anï¿½ I wish I was deadï¿½
Didnï¿½t you kiss me anï¿½ promise? you havenï¿½t done it, my lad,
Anï¿½ I almost died oï¿½ your going away, anï¿½ I wish that I had.ï¿½

XII.
I too wish that I hadï¿½in the pleasant times that had past,
Before I quarrellï¿½d with Harryï¿½my quarrelï¿½the first anï¿½ the last.

XIII.
For Harry came in, anï¿½ I flung him the letter that drove me wild,
Anï¿½ he told it me all at once, as simple as any child,
ï¿½What can it matter, my lass, what I did wiï¿½ my single life?
I haï¿½ been as true to you as ever a man to his wife;
Anï¿½ she wasnï¿½t one oï¿½ the worst.ï¿½ ï¿½Then,ï¿½ I said, ï¿½Iï¿½m none oï¿½ the best.ï¿½
Anï¿½ he smiled at me, ï¿½Ainï¿½t you, my love? Come, come, little wife, let it rest!
The man isnï¿½t like the woman, no need to make such a stir.ï¿½
But he angerï¿½d me all the more, anï¿½ I said ï¿½You were keeping with her,
When I was a-loving you all along anï¿½ the same as before.ï¿½
Anï¿½ he didnï¿½t speak for a while, anï¿½ he angerï¿½d me more and more.
ï¿½Then he patted my hand in his gentle way, ï¿½Let bygones be!ï¿½
ï¿½Bygones! you kept yours hushï¿½d,ï¿½ I said, ï¿½when you married me!
By-gones maï¿½ be come-agains; anï¿½ sheï¿½in her shame anï¿½ her sinï¿½
Youï¿½ll have her to nurse my child, if I die oï¿½ my lying in!
Youï¿½ll make her its second mother! I hate herï¿½anï¿½ I hate you!ï¿½
Ah, Harry, my man, you had better haï¿½ beaten me black anï¿½ blue
Than haï¿½ spoken as kind as you did, when I were so crazy wiï¿½ spite,
ï¿½Wait a little, my lass, I am sure it ï¿½ill all come right.ï¿½

XIV.
Anï¿½ he took three turns in the rain, anï¿½ I watchï¿½d him, anï¿½ when he came in
I felt that my heart was hard, he was all wet throï¿½ to the skin,
Anï¿½ I never said ï¿½off wiï¿½ the wet,ï¿½ I never said ï¿½on wiï¿½ the dry,ï¿½
So I knew my heart was hard, when he came to bid me goodbye.
ï¿½You said that you hated me, Ellen, but that isnï¿½t true, you know;
I am going to leave you a bitï¿½youï¿½ll kiss me before I go?ï¿½

XV.
ï¿½Going! youï¿½re going to herï¿½kiss herï¿½if you will,ï¿½ I saidï¿½
I was near my time wiï¿½ the boy, I must haï¿½ been light iï¿½ my headï¿½
ï¿½I had sooner be cursed than kissï¿½d!ï¿½ï¿½I didnï¿½t know well what I meant,
But I turnï¿½d my face from him, anï¿½ he turnï¿½d his face anï¿½ he went.

XV1.
And then he sent me a letter, ï¿½Iï¿½ve gotten my work to do;
You wouldnï¿½t kiss me, my lass, anï¿½ I never loved any but you;
I am sorry for all the quarrel anï¿½ sorry for what she wrote,
I haï¿½ six weeksï¿½ work in Jersey anï¿½ go to-night by the boat.ï¿½

XVII.
Anï¿½ the wind began to rise, anï¿½ I thought of him out at sea,
Anï¿½ I felt I had been to blame; he was always kind to me.
ï¿½Wait a little, my lass, I am sure it ï¿½ill all come rightï¿½ï¿½
Anï¿½ the boat went down that nightï¿½the boat went down that night.
meaning:
About: Alfred Tennyson, 1st Baron Tennyson (6 August 1809 ï¿½ 6 October 1892) was Poet Laureate of the United Kingdom and is one of the most popular English poets.

Much of his verse was based on classical mythological themes, although In Memoriam was written to commemorate his best friend Arthur Hallam, a fellow poet and classmate at Trinity College, Cambridge who was engaged to Tennyson's sister but died from a cerebral hemorrhage. One of Tennyson's most famous works is Idylls of the King (1885), a series of narrative poems based entirely on King Arthur and the Arthurian tales, as thematically suggested by Sir Thomas Malory's earlier tales on the legendary king. The work was dedicated to Prince Albert, the husband of Queen Victoria. During his career, Lord Tennyson attempted drama, but his plays enjoyed little success even in his lifetime.

Tennyson wrote a number of phrases that have become commonplaces of the English language, including: "nature, red in tooth and claw", "better to have loved and lost", "Theirs not to reason why, Theirs but to do and die", and "My strength is as the strength of ten, Because my heart is pure".

He is the second most frequently quoted writer in the English language, after Shakespeare.
